data.title: Buffett's Berkshire Hathaway exits Citi, cuts BofA, COF stakes, among other Q1 moves
data.description:
Warren Buffett's Berkshire Hathaway (NYSE:BRK.B) (BRK.A) continued to lighten its holdings of banks and loaded up on Constellation Brands (STZ) in Q1 2025, according to its latest 13F filing.

The insurance company with a hefty securities portfolio divested the rest of its 14.6M shares in Citigroup (C) and 40.2M shares of Nubank (NU).

It reduced its holdings in Bank of America (BAC) to 631.6M shares ($26.4B) from 680.2M, Capital One Financial (COF) to 7.15M shares ($1.28B) from 7.45M, and DaVita (DVA) to 35.1M shares ($5.38B) from 36.1M, among other moves.

Increased stakes included: Constellation Brands (STZ) to 12.0M shares, valued at $2.2B at March 31, 2025, from M shares; VeriSign to 13.3M shares ($3.37B) from M shares; and Pool Corp. (POOL) to 1.46M shares ($466.1M) from M shares. 

Notably, Berkshire (NYSE:BRK.B) held its Apple (AAPL) stake steady at 300M shares ($66.6B) during the quarter, according to the filing.

data.title: MDU Resources Group appoints new board chair
data.description:
• MDU Resources Group (NYSE:MDU) has elected Darrel T. Anderson as its new independent chair of the board, succeeding Dennis W. Johnson, effective immediately.
  • Johnson will remain on the board as a director and will continue to serve as chair of the Nominating and Governance committee.
  • Anderson joined the board in 2023, and previously served as board vice chair and chair of the Compensation and Human Capital committee.  
  • Source: Press release

data.title: Modular Medical files $75M mixed securities shelf
data.description:
• Modular Medical (NASDAQ:MODD) filed a prospectus for the offer and sale of $75M mixed securities shelf. 
  • This prospectus is not an offer to sell these securities. 
  • Filing.

data.title: SA Asks: Would Trump's drug price cuts hurt biotech more than pharma?
data.description:
Would the cost-cutting measures for U.S. prescription drugs proposed by the Trump administration on Monday hurt the biotech sector more than pharma?

We asked Seeking Alpha analysts Stephen Ayers and Edmund Ingham for their views.

Stephen Ayers: Pharmaceutical companies are more directly impacted by the proposed cuts, but they have the resources to withstand some pressure. The impact on biotechnology companies is more nuanced, but these changes will be reflected in valuations. If the pricing power is reduced across the board for drugs, a Phase 2 investigational drug’s net present value, or NPV, for example, would be lower than in the past, reducing the valuation of a biotechnology company.  

Edmund Ingham: Right now, the proposed cuts to drug prices have - somewhat bizarrely - brought buoyancy to biotech and pharma stocks - the biotech ETF (NYSEARCA:XBI) gained >4% on Monday, as did stock prices of most Big Pharma companies.  

This may reflect one of two things - firstly, that pharmas and biotechs are not taking the president seriously - after all, these same proposed policy changes were easily defeated in President Trump’s first term in office by the pharma industry. Secondly, the biotech sector is suffering, and has been for some time. Having hit rock bottom, perhaps the industry is prepared to welcome change of any kind. 

  • Top Pharmaceuticals Stocks
  • Top Biotechnology Stocks

data.title: Janus International Group increases share buyback program by $75M
data.description:
• Janus International Group (NYSE:JBI) has expanded JBI’s existing share repurchase program to purchase up to an additional $75M of its common stock.
  • The prior authorization was for $100M.
  • As of March 29, 2025, JBI has repurchased ~7.8M shares of common stock at $83.7M.
  • Source: Press release

data.title: Globus Medical announces share buyback program of up to $500M
data.description:
• Globus Medical (NYSE:GMED) has authorized a share repurchase program of up to $500M of GMED’s outstanding common stock.
  • GMED shares up over 1% post-market on Thursday.
  • Source: Press release

data.title: HHS to drop routine COVID shots recommendation for children, pregnant women - WSJ
data.description:
The Department of Health and Human Services is expected to drop recommendations for routine COVID-19 vaccination for pregnant women, teenagers and children, the Wall Street Journal reported on Thursday.

This news comes amidst FDA Commissioner Marty Makary's announcement that the agency is preparing a new regulatory framework clarifying its guidance related to vaccines, particularly COVID-19 shots.

According to the WSJ report citing people familiar with the matter, the HHS is expected to remove the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention's (CDC) recommendations for the specified groups at the same time it also launches a new vaccine framework.

As per the current recommendation by the CDC, which is a part of the HHS, everyone six months and older, including pregnant women, should receive Covid-19 vaccines.

The Trump administration's recent hires to the health department, including HHS Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr., have been critical of the COVID-19 vaccine shots with messenger-RNA technology.

Moderna (NASDAQ:MRNA), Pfizer (NYSE:PFE), Biontech (NASDAQ:BNTX) and Novavax (NASDAQ:NVAX) are among some big names with COVID-19 vaccine authorizations.

The exact date of this announcement is yet to be confirmed, but it is expected in the upcoming days, the report added.

data.title: Arena Group GAAP EPS of $0.08, revenue of $31.8M
data.description:
• Arena Group press release (NYSE:AREN): Q1 GAAP EPS of $0.08.

  • Income from continuing operations was $4.0M.
  • Financial Guidance for Q2 2025: Revenue of around $40-$45M.
  • Income from continuing operations of about $9 - $11M.

data.title: Pentagon prepares framework for ‘Golden Dome’ defense project amid concerns
data.description:
The Pentagon completed an initial blueprint for the proposed “Golden Dome” air and missile defense system, according to the Department of Defense, as questions continue to surface regarding the feasibility and cost of the initiative promoted by the Trump administration.

The defense plan follows a January 27 executive order from President Donald Trump that directed the Pentagon to explore comprehensive options to enhance the nation’s missile defense capabilities.

In a statement to Bloomberg News, spokesperson Sean Parnell said the department enlisted top defense experts and engineers to assess both existing U.S. missile defense systems and emerging technologies. The objective, he added, is to rapidly build a “reliable umbrella of protection” for the United States.

Parnell noted that Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth and senior military officials have briefed the president on potential strategies and anticipate providing a formal update on the project's direction in the near future.

During a White House press event on Monday -- primarily focused on a separate executive order to lower prescription drug costs -- an image promoting the Golden Dome concept was displayed. President Trump claimed the expected savings from the healthcare initiative would offset the defense system’s cost, though no financial specifics were disclosed.

Prior to the Pentagon’s latest comments, neither the Department of Defense nor the White House had shared technical specifications, development timelines or budget projections for the Golden Dome effort. That lack of transparency has raised concerns among lawmakers involved in authorizing defense funding.

An analysis by the Congressional Budget Office highlights the financial stakes, estimating that the most controversial portion of the program -- space-based interceptors -- could cost anywhere between $161 billion and $542 billion over the next two decades. The final cost will depend on the scale of deployment and associated launch expenses, according to a CBO report prepared for a Senate Armed Services subcommittee.

Meanwhile, the Defense Intelligence Agency released an updated threat assessment identifying missile threats the system is designed to counter. Lieutenant General Jeffrey Kruse, DIA director, told lawmakers in written testimony that the agency is supplying essential intelligence to guide the system’s design and implementation.

The Golden Dome remains in its early stages, but its scope and cost are likely to remain key points of debate as the administration moves forward with the initiative.

Several U.S. companies have expressed interest in contributing to the development of the Golden Dome missile defense system.

Lockheed Martin (NYSE:LMT) publicly announced its readiness to support the Golden Dome project. The company is evaluating various assets, including its command-and-control networks, F-35 stealth fighter and Sentinel A4 radar, to support the initiative. Lockheed Martin's (NYSE:LMT) technology, like the TPY-6 radar and the PAC-3 Missile Segment Enhancement, has shown success in recent tests.

SpaceX (SPACE), led by Elon Musk, has emerged as a frontrunner to assist in developing the Golden Dome missile defense system. The company is collaborating with data analytics firm Palantir (NASDAQ:PLTR) and defense company Anduril to build crucial components of the system. Their plan includes launching 400 to more than 1,000 satellites for tracking, alongside a separate fleet of 200 attack satellites potentially armed with missiles or lasers. However, SpaceX (SPACE) is expected to focus solely on satellite tracking, not weaponization.

BlueHalo, the defense technology company acquired by AV (NASDAQ:AVAV), formerly known as AeroVironment, sees an opportunity for the Golden Dome project to encompass more than missile defense. The company advocates for integrating counter-drone capabilities and electronic warfare into the system, emphasizing the need for a comprehensive approach to modern threats.

RTX (NYSE:RTX), Northrop Grumman (NYSE:NOC) and General Dynamics (NYSE:GD) also are among the traditional defense contractors expressing interest in the Golden Dome initiative.

data.title: Comstock appoints new CFO
data.description:
• Comstock (NYSE:LODE) has approved the appointment of Judd B. Merrill, as chief financial officer of the company and president of LODE’s wholly-owned mining subsidiary, Comstock Mining.
  • Merrill will assume his new role starting May 19, 2025.
  • Merrill, most recently served as CFO of Aqua Metals (AQMS).
  • Source: Press release
